title: Allergy Testing: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Differentiate skin prick testing from intradermal testing indications
Interpret specific IgE levels and their clinical significance
Apply component-resolved diagnostics in ambiguous cases

sections.title: Skin Prick Testing (SPT)
sections.content:
**Gold standard for IgE-mediated allergy diagnosis**

• Introduces allergen extracts into epidermis via lancet
• Wheal ≥3mm greater than negative control = positive
• Results in 15-20 minutes
• High sensitivity (~90%) but lower specificity (~50%)
• Must hold antihistamines 3-7 days prior

**Contraindications:**
• Severe dermatographism
• Widespread eczema at test sites
• Recent anaphylaxis (wait 4-6 weeks)
• Unable to stop antihistamines

sections.title: Serum Specific IgE (sIgE)
sections.content:
**Alternative when skin testing not feasible**

• Not affected by antihistamines or skin conditions
• Quantitative result (kU/L)
• Class 0 (<0.35): Negative
• Class 1 (0.35-0.7): Equivocal
• Class 2-6 (>0.7): Positive with increasing probability

**Key Limitations:**
• Measures sensitization, NOT clinical allergy
• Cross-reactivity with homologous proteins
• Lower sensitivity than SPT for some allergens

sections.title: Component-Resolved Diagnostics
sections.content:
**Identifies specific allergenic proteins**

Examples:
• Peanut: Ara h 2 (high risk anaphylaxis) vs Ara h 8 (oral allergy, low risk)
• Milk: Casein (persistent allergy) vs whey (may outgrow)
• Bee venom: Api m 1 vs Api m 10 (distinguishes true vs cross-reactive)

**Clinical Impact:**
• Reduces unnecessary avoidance
• Predicts severity of reactions
• Guides immunotherapy candidacy

sections.title: Oral Food Challenges
sections.content:
**Definitive test for food allergy**

• Double-blind placebo-controlled (DBPCFC) = gold standard
• Open challenges used clinically
• Graded doses over 2-4 hours
• Must have emergency equipment available
• Indicated when history and testing are discordant

mcqs.stem: A patient undergoes skin prick testing to peanut. The wheal is 8mm with a negative control of 0mm and histamine control of 5mm. How should this result be interpreted?

options.text: Positive test — wheal ≥3mm greater than negative control indicates IgE sensitization
options.explanation: Per AAAAI/ACAAI practice parameters, a wheal ≥3mm greater than the negative control is considered positive. This indicates IgE sensitization but must be correlated with clinical history to diagnose true allergy.

options.text: Negative test — wheal must exceed histamine control to be positive
options.explanation: The positive threshold is compared to the NEGATIVE control (≥3mm difference), not the histamine control. The histamine control validates that the patient can mount a response.

options.text: Equivocal — requires serum specific IgE for confirmation
options.explanation: An 8mm wheal with 0mm negative control is clearly positive (8mm difference). Serum sIgE may complement but is not required for this interpretation.

options.text: Invalid test — the histamine control is too small
options.explanation: A histamine wheal of ≥3mm validates adequate skin reactivity. A 5mm histamine response is normal and validates the test.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Positive test — wheal ≥3mm greater than negative control indicates IgE sensitization.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Positive test — wheal ≥3mm greater than negative control indicates IgE sensitization

coachDx.misconception: A histamine wheal of ≥3mm validates adequate skin reactivity. A 5mm histamine response is normal and validates the test.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Positive test — wheal ≥3mm greater than negative control indicates IgE sensitiza from Invalid test — the histamine control is too small.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Positive test — wheal ≥3mm greater than negative control indicates IgE sensitization.
